chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Welcome back. Let me now show you the solution for the responsive navbar exercise.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: First, we create the HTML structure with a nav element containing a logo div and a ul with links.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: We also add a hamburger icon div that will be hidden on desktop.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: For the CSS, we set display flex on the nav and justify-content space-between.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: The ul also gets display flex to arrange the links horizontally.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: For the mobile version, we add a media query at max-width 768px.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Inside the media query, we change the flex-direction of the nav to column.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: We also hide and show the hamburger icon using display none and display block.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Adding a smooth transition on the ul height makes the menu appear with animation.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: This is a very common pattern you will see in real-world websites.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: I also want to share some CSS tips that will help you write better CSS.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Always use CSS custom properties, also called CSS variables, for colors and common values.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Define them in the root selector and use them throughout your stylesheet.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Use BEM naming convention for your CSS classes to keep your code organized.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: BEM stands for Block Element Modifier and it helps avoid naming conflicts.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: Also learn to use the CSS calc function for dynamic calculations.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: For example, calc(100vh - 60px) subtracts the navbar height from the viewport height.

chunks.title: Exercise 2 Solution & More CSS Tips
chunks.text: These practices will make your CSS code much more maintainable and professional.

text: Welcome back. Let me now show you the solution for the responsive navbar exercise. First, we create the HTML structure with a nav element containing a logo div and a ul with links. We also add a hamburger icon div that will be hidden on desktop. For the CSS, we set display flex on the nav and justify-content space-between. The ul also gets display flex to arrange the links horizontally. For the mobile version, we add a media query at max-width 768px. Inside the media query, we change the flex-direction of the nav to column. We also hide and show the hamburger icon using display none and display block. Adding a smooth transition on the ul height makes the menu appear with animation. This is a very common pattern you will see in real-world websites. I also want to share some CSS tips that will help you write better CSS. Always use CSS custom properties, also called CSS variables, for colors and common values. Define them in the root selector and use them throughout your stylesheet. Use BEM naming convention for your CSS classes to keep your code organized. BEM stands for Block Element Modifier and it helps avoid naming conflicts. Also learn to use the CSS calc function for dynamic calculations. For example, calc(100vh - 60px) subtracts the navbar height from the viewport height. These practices will make your CSS code much more maintainable and professional.
